case_name: Strychnine control in surface water for municipal drinking water with minimal chemical use
task_description: Recommend a ranked list of feasible treatment process chains for strychnine-contaminated surface water intended for drinking water production under a minimal-chemical-use preference.

input_data.scenario_background: A small town draws raw water from a river intake downstream of agricultural and pest-control storage areas. After a local spill event, strychnine has been detected at levels requiring active removal before the water can be used for municipal drinking water supply. The utility prefers established treatment approaches that minimize routine chemical addition while still providing reliable control under variable surface-water conditions.

treatment_goal.target_requirement: Finished water should achieve effective strychnine reduction suitable for drinking water production and also provide normal particulate and microbial control expected for potable supply.
secondary_goals: use proven drinking water treatment technology
maintain manageable operation for a small municipal utility
avoid unnecessarily high-energy treatment where simpler options are adequate

engineering_constraints.residuals_management: backwash water handling and limited spent media changeout are manageable, but high-volume hazardous concentrate or excessive sludge production is undesirable
engineering_constraints.robustness_requirement: reliable performance is needed despite seasonal turbidity and natural organic matter variation in the river

core_unit_functions.intake_screening: remove large debris and protect downstream equipment
core_unit_functions.media_filtration: reduce turbidity and suspended solids to protect the adsorber
core_unit_functions.granular_activated_carbon_adsorption: adsorb strychnine and other hydrophobic or adsorbable organic micropollutants
core_unit_functions.disinfection: provide final microbiological protection for drinking water

applicability_rationale: strychnine is an organic micropollutant for which activated carbon is a well-established control approach in drinking water treatment
surface water has moderate turbidity and natural organic matter, so particulate removal ahead of GAC helps preserve adsorption capacity
this process chain aligns well with the stated preference for minimal routine chemical use because adsorption and filtration can be operated with limited chemical addition
the train is practical for a small municipal utility and avoids the higher complexity of advanced oxidation or high-pressure membrane systems
potential_risks: natural organic matter can compete for adsorption sites and shorten GAC bed life
breakthrough monitoring is required to ensure timely media replacement or reactivation
if raw-water turbidity spikes are not controlled, headloss and carbon fouling may increase

core_unit_functions.intake_screening: remove coarse solids at the intake
core_unit_functions.powdered_activated_carbon_contact: provide rapid adsorption of dissolved strychnine in the water column
core_unit_functions.clarification_or_ballasted_settling: separate PAC-associated solids and suspended matter
core_unit_functions.filtration: polish remaining particles and fine carbon carryover
core_unit_functions.disinfection: ensure microbiological safety of finished water

applicability_rationale: PAC is a recognized emergency and seasonal response option for episodic organic contaminant events in surface water
the contamination was linked to a spill event, so a flexible adsorption step is attractive when concentrations may decline over time
this approach can achieve strychnine reduction without relying on intensive oxidation chemistry
it is appropriate when the utility already operates conventional solids removal
potential_risks: PAC performance depends strongly on dose, contact time, and NOM competition
routine chemical use is higher than GAC because PAC must be fed continuously during the event
additional solids handling is required for carbon-bearing residuals

core_unit_functions.intake_screening: remove debris and protect process units
core_unit_functions.ozonation: oxidize strychnine and transform organic contaminants into more biodegradable compounds
core_unit_functions.biological_activated_carbon: adsorb remaining organics and biologically polish oxidation byproducts
core_unit_functions.filtration: remove detached biomass and residual particulates

applicability_rationale: combined ozone and biologically active carbon is a proven advanced barrier for difficult organic micropollutants in surface water treatment
the BAC step helps manage oxidation byproducts while also improving total organic control
this train can be robust where raw-water matrix effects reduce carbon-only performance
it remains more established for drinking water than some more specialized destructive technologies
potential_risks: uses more energy and operational oversight than adsorption-only options
ozone performance and byproduct formation depend on water matrix and bromide, even if bromide is not elevated here
biological filter management requires careful startup and monitoring

core_unit_functions.intake_screening: remove coarse material before membrane pretreatment
core_unit_functions.ultrafiltration: reduce suspended solids and protect the high-pressure membrane
core_unit_functions.nanofiltration_or_reverse_osmosis: provide strong rejection of dissolved organic contaminants including strychnine
core_unit_functions.stabilization: adjust permeate chemistry for distribution compatibility
core_unit_functions.disinfection: maintain microbiological safety in finished water

applicability_rationale: high-pressure membranes can technically provide a strong barrier for dissolved organic contaminants
the process can also address broader water quality issues beyond strychnine if needed
it is a credible fallback when very high removal assurance is prioritized
potential_risks: concentrate management is burdensome for a small utility
membrane fouling risk is significant with surface-water NOM and turbidity unless pretreatment is strong
this option conflicts with the desire to avoid unnecessarily complex and energy-intensive treatment

core_unit_functions.advanced_oxidation_process: generate highly reactive oxidants to degrade strychnine
core_unit_functions.biological_filtration: remove oxidation byproducts and biodegradable residual organics
core_unit_functions.disinfection: ensure final potable water microbiological safety

applicability_rationale: advanced oxidation can destroy certain persistent organic contaminants when adsorption alone is insufficient
the downstream biological step can help polish oxidation products
this is technically plausible but generally more specialized than necessary for a straightforward single-contaminant case
potential_risks: chemical and energy demand are high relative to the stated minimal-chemical-use objective
incomplete oxidation may create intermediate products requiring additional control
process optimization is more complex than conventional adsorption-based treatment
